questions.question: What is leakage in a superconducting transmon qubit?

options.text: Population escaping to higher energy states beyond |0⟩ and |1⟩

questions.explanation: Leakage occurs when the qubit's population escapes from the computational subspace (|0⟩ and |1⟩) to higher energy states like |2⟩. Stabilizer codes can't correct this, so special leakage detection and reset is needed.

questions.question: Why is frequency allocation important in superconducting QEC chips?

options.text: To avoid frequency collisions that cause unwanted coupling and crosstalk

questions.explanation: Adjacent qubits with similar frequencies experience unwanted ZZ coupling (always-on interaction), causing gate errors and crosstalk. Using a pattern of different frequencies prevents these collisions.

questions.question: What is the typical duration of one syndrome extraction round on superconducting qubits?
questions.explanation: A typical syndrome round takes about 1 microsecond, including gate operations (~300 ns), measurement (~500 ns), and reset (~200 ns). This must fit within the coherence time for multiple QEC cycles.

questions.question: What is a Leakage Reduction Unit (LRU)?

options.text: A device that cools the qubits
A circuit that detects and resets qubits that have leaked to higher states
A type of error correction code

questions.explanation: An LRU is a circuit added to the QEC cycle that checks if a qubit has leaked to states beyond |1⟩ and resets it back to the computational subspace if so.

questions.question: What causes crosstalk errors in superconducting processors?

options.text: Stray capacitive coupling and microwave drive leakage between nearby qubits

questions.explanation: Crosstalk arises from unintended interactions between qubits, including stray capacitive coupling, microwave control pulses affecting nearby qubits, and resonator-mediated coupling.

questions.question: In surface code chip layout, where are data and ancilla qubits placed?

options.text: Data at vertices, ancillas at face centers in alternating X/Z pattern
All data on one side, all ancilla on the other
Only data qubits are on the chip

questions.explanation: In the surface code chip layout, data qubits are placed at vertices of a square lattice, while ancilla qubits for X and Z stabilizer measurements are placed at alternating face centers.

questions.question: Measurement errors in superconducting QEC can be mitigated by repeating syndrome measurements multiple times and using 3D decoding.
questions.explanation: True. Repeated measurements help distinguish real data errors (which persist) from transient measurement errors (which appear and disappear). The 3D decoder uses the time dimension to identify and filter measurement errors.
